coredata.dc:title: Anthrax biomarker: An ultrasensitive fluorescent ratiometry of dipicolinic acid by using terbium(III)-modified carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meng Li
Chen, Bin Bin
He, Jia Hui
Li, Chun Mei
Li, Yuan Fang
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: The highly sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker of the biological threat-agent anthrax, is strongly associated with the sensing of Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis) in environmental and food samples. In this study, we developed a novel, ultrasensitive method for the detection of DPA by using a ratiometric fluorescent terbium ions modified carbon dots (CDs-Tb). The CDs-Tb showed two fluorescent emission bands at 459 nm and 495 nm when excited at the single wavelength of 260 nm. DPA could specifically bind with terbium ions on the surface of CDs through strong chelate-conjugation to produce antenna effect, resulting in significantly enhancement of the 495 nm emission peak without affecting the 459 nm emission peak. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of CDs-Tb was proportional to the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.5 nM to 2.5 μM with the limit of detection as low as 100 pM. This selective and ultrasensitive assay had a great application prospect in the complex matrixes owing to its simplicity and specificity for DPA. Meanwhile, the CDs-Tb-based paper sensor was successfully developed for sensitive and visual detection of DPA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dipicolinic acid (DPA)
Anthrax biomarker
Terbium(III) modified carbon dots (CDs-Tb)
Fluorescence ratiometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs-Tb-based paper sensor for DPA visual detection
Characteristic features of the CDs and CDs-Tb
Determination of DPA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and FL spectra of CDs. (a) HRTEM image; (b) Particle size distribution; (c) HRTEM image and (d) FL spectra of CDs.
FL spectra of CDs-Tb. (a) FL emission spectra of CDs-Tb; (b) FL spectra of CDs before and after adding Tb3+ ions.

The sensitivity of DPA detection. (a) The change of the FL spectra of CDs-Tb in the presence of DPA; (b) The sensitivity of DPA detection (Inset: CDs-Tb solution in the absence (Left) and presence (Right) of 50 μM DPA under the 254 nm UV light lamp). c CDs-Tb, 50 μg/ml; pH, 7.02 (20 mM Tris-HCl); EX: 260 nm.

The mechanism of DPA detection. (a) FL responses of the CDs in the absence and presence of DPA. c CDs, 50 μg/ml; (b) FL responses of the Tb3+ ions (1 mM) in the absence and presence of DPA. c DPA, 50 μM.

The selectivity of DPA detection. (a) FL responses of the CDs-Tb in the absence and presence of 10 μM interferents. 1, Control; 2, Cl-; 3, PO4 3-; 4, HCO3 -; 5, SO4 2-; 6, L-Cys; 7, G-SH; 8, Vc; 9, BA; 10, CA; 11, ATP; 12, UTP; 13, CTP; 14, GTP. (b) FL responses of the CDs-Tb in the absence and presence of 10 μM amino acid. c CDs-Tb, 50 μg/ml; c DPA, 1 μM; pH, 7.02 (20 mM Tris-HCl); EX: 260 nm.

CDs-Tb-based paper sensor for DPA visual detection. Photographs of the paper test strips staining with DPA at different concentrations (from 5000 to 0.5 nm) under (a) daylight and (b) 254 nm UV lamp. (c) The 3D models of the images in (b).
Illustration of the ratiometric fluorescent detection of DPA by using CDs-Tb.
The comparison of the determination of DPA.
Determination of DPA in two samples. pH: 7.02; EX: 260 nm; c CDs-Tb: 50 μg/ml.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample 1 is orange juice; sample 2 is running water.

head.ce:title: Anthrax biomarker: An ultrasensitive fluorescent ratiometry of dipicolinic acid by using terbium(III)-modified carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker of the biological threat-agent anthrax, is strongly associated with the sensing of Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis) in environmental and food samples. In this study, we developed a novel, ultrasensitive method for the detection of DPA by using a ratiometric fluorescent terbium ions modified carbon dots (CDs-Tb). The CDs-Tb showed two fluorescent emission bands at 459 nm and 495 nm when excited at the single wavelength of 260 nm. DPA could specifically bind with terbium ions on the surface of CDs through strong chelate-conjugation to produce antenna effect, resulting in significantly enhancement of the 495 nm emission peak without affecting the 459 nm emission peak. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of CDs-Tb was proportional to the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.5 nM to 2.5 μM with the limit of detection as low as 100 pM. This selective and ultrasensitive assay had a great application prospect in the complex matrixes owing to its simplicity and specificity for DPA. Meanwhile, the CDs-Tb-based paper sensor was successfully developed for sensitive and visual detection of DPA.

Terbium(III)-modified carbon dots (CDs-Tb) for ultrasensitive and specific detection of DPA by using fluorescence ratio method.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an acute communicable disease caused by Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis), of which the spores can be applied as a biological warfare agent and seriously threaten public safety [1–3]. The inhalation of > 104 B. anthracis spores is lethal, unless medical treatment is received within 24–48 h [4,5]. Infections can occur through eating, breathing, or skin exposure [6]. Therefore, the fast and efficient detection of such spores is critical for prevention and control of the anthrax disease and one way to cut the throat of bioterrorism. As one of the major components of B. anthracis spores (5–15% of dry mass), dipicolinic acid (DPA) has been commonly adopted as the only one unique biomarker to identify the anthrax disease or B. anthracis contamination [7].

In the hope to make early warning signs of B. anthracis contamination, methods involving mass spectrometry [8], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6,9], electrochemical methods [10], and spectrofluorometric methods [11,15] have been developed for the identification and detection of DPA, since they are sensitive and accurate. Particularly, spectrofluorometric methods have been widely applied by developing new fluorescent chromophore such as carbazole-2,7-dicarboxamide [11], PVA film [12], dual lanthanide-doped complexes [13], and bio-metal-organic framework [14], etc. Although these methods are low-cost, facile, rapid and highly selective, their limit of detection (LOD) usually reach only tens or hundreds of nanomolar, which cannot meet the requirement of the small quantity of B. anthracis that can cause death (104 spores) [15], therefore, developing a highly sensitive way for detecting DPA is valuable and critical to the real needs.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescence (FL) chromophores with excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and easy preparation have been widely applied in the analytical field [16–25]. Generally, CDs is composed of the core with sp2-hybidized carbon clusters and the shell with sp3-hybidized carbons, which has larger Stokes shift and better water solubility compared with organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [26–28]. The FL properties of CDs are closely related with surface defects in the shell [29,30] or intrinsic quantization effect in the nanocrystalline core [31]. In order to design and prepare the CDs-based nanoprobes with better properties, we ingeniously utilize the antenna effect of rare earth ions, and combine it on the surface of the CDs, developing a ratio fluorescent nanoprobe with two luminescent centers. The luminescence efficiency of the rare earth ions itself is very low, but when it combines with the ligand molecules, there would be an enhanced FL. Ligands may absorb light at a frequency characteristic of the ligand and transfer it to the triplet states of the rare earth ion [32], thus greatly improving the luminescence efficiency of the rare earth ions.

In this work, we demonstrated a novel, ultrasensitive DPA nano-sensing platform based on ratiometric fluorescent terbium ions modified CDs (CDs-Tb). The emission peak at 459 nm of the CDs in aqueous solution remained unchanged, thus serving as an internal reference; while the FL emission peak at 495 nm of Tb3+ exhibited sharply enhancement upon binding with DPA, thus serving as the signal reporter ( Scheme 1). The fluorescent ratiometry not only improved the detection sensitivity but also avoided interference from the background FL. The FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of the CDs-Tb correlated linearly with the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.5 nM to 2.5 μM with a LOD of 100 pM. Meanwhile, the CDs-Tb-based paper sensor was successfully developed for DPA visual detection, and the result showed high sensitivity even when the concentration of DPA as low as 0.5 nM.

Terbium (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3·5H2O), DPA, glutathione (G-SH), triethylenetetramine (TETA) and L-cysteine (L-cys) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent. Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Vitamin C (Vc) was purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Benzoic acid (BA) was purchased from Chuandong Chemical Group Co., Ltd (Chongqing, China). Tris-HCl buffer was adopted to adjust the ionic strength and control the acidity. Milli-Q purified water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare solutions throughout the experiment.

The TEM data of the CDs and CDs-Tb were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The FL spectra of CDs-Tb were recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). All water used was ultrapurified with an LD-50G-E Ultra-Pure Water System (Lidi Modern Waters Equipments Co., Chongqing, China).

The CD-Tb were prepared by a facile two-step method based on our previous method [33]. Shortly, 100 mg of citric acid and 200 μl TETA were added in to 5.0 ml of water simultaneously. After mixed fully, the mixture was transferred into a 25 ml Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave, heating at 160 °C for 3 h. The CDs with rich carboxyl and anime groups were obtained through the hydrothermal reaction. Then, the CDs solution (10 ml, 0.3 mg/ml) was mixed with aqueous Tb(NO3)3 solution (5 ml, 0.1 M) for 3 h at room temperature. The terbium ions (Tb3+) could bind with carboxyl and anime groups on the surface of CDs to produce CDs-Tb. The resulting mixture was then dialyzed with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (100–500 MWCO) for 4 days to remove “free” Tb3+. Finally, the pure CDs-Tb were prepared.

Titration of CDs-Tb with DPA was made by adding various concentrations of DPA (0–2.5 μM) into the CDs-Tb solution (50 μg/ml, Tris-HCl buffer, pH = 7.02), and then subjected to FL measurements after 5 min. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-Tb-based paper sensor for DPA visual detection
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the CDs solution (10 ml, 0.3 mg/ml) was mixed with aqueous Tb(NO3)3 solution (5 ml, 0.1 M) for 3 h at room temperature. Then the filter papers strips were immersed in the mixed solution for one minute and heated to dry. Repeat the step over 5 times. Drop-wising DPA solution (100 μl) with different concentrations onto the filter papers strips and then making them dry. Finally, those strips were illuminated with a commercial 254 nm UV lamp to observe the FL with the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic features of the CDs and CDs-Tb
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-Tb could be easily prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, the blue-emissive CDs were prepared through hydrothermal carbonization by using citric acid and TETA as the reaction precursor. The reaction precursors experienced the processes of condensation and carbonization at high temperature. The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) result (Fig. S1) showed the ν C-H bands in the 2917 and 2850 cm−1 regions as well as the ν O-H, ν C=O, ν N-H, ν C-N, and ν C-O bands at 3447, 1640, 1566, 1394 and 1095 cm−1, which confirmed the presence of amide, amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. That is, the N-H of TETA and -COOH of citric acid suffered from the amidation reaction at high temperature.

The TEM image (Fig. S2) showed that the formed CDs were typical spherical structure. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image ( Fig. 1a) of CDs clearly displayed that the CDs were well mono-dispersed. Their size distribution ranged from 3 nm to 9 nm (Fig. 1b) with maximum population at about 5.69 nm ± 2.65 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) further showed that the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes was 0.19 nm and 0.33 nm, which respectively corresponded to the (020) and (002) planes of graphitic carbon, indicating that the CDs possessed a graphite-like structure [3,29]. It is note that the lattice parameters are different with both the intra- and inter-plane lattice parameter (0.214 and 0.340–0.403 nm, respectively) of the reported CDs coming from carbon precursor without nitrogen [34]. The mismatch of lattice parameter may owe to the doping of nitrogen [35].

The as-obtained CDs in aqueous solution showed two absorption peaks in 240 and 354 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). This peak at 240 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, resulted in nearly no observed FL signal. The another peak at 354 nm, due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states, resulted in strong emission [36]. The blue emissive CDs showed excitation-independent emission property and the maximum emission could be available at 463 nm with the maximum excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 1d), indicating that the FL properties of the resulting CDs depended on the surface state because the size of CDs was not very uniform (3–9 nm) [35,36]. Furthermore, the excitation spectra (Fig. S4) of CDs were found to only have one peak and were invariant with different emission wavelength in the range of 430–480 nm. In other words, the single transition mode with a certain energy, taking place only through the radiative transition of sp2 carbon, was responsible for the excitation independence properties of CDs [37]. The invariability of the emission wavelength is beneficial for applications in chemosensing and bioimaging.

Then, by an easy mixture of CDs and Tb(NO3)3 solution, the CDs-Tb were produced. Under the excitation of 310–380 nm light beam, the CDs-Tb showed excitation wavelength independent FL emissions, and the maximum emission was located at 459 nm with the maximum excitation at 350 nm ( Fig. 2a). Although the FL properties of CDs-Tb displayed an unobvious variation in comparison to the original CDs, their luminescence efficiency enhanced. The CDs had a high quantum yield (QY) of 32.9% measured by a Quantaurus-QY. The high QY of CDs was because the N-doping introduced a new surface state labeled as the N-state [36]. Electrons trapped by the new formed surface state can produce a high yield of radiative recombination. The introduction of Tb3+ further enhanced the FL of CDs (Fig. 2b) because the bind of Tb3+ ions with the surface groups of CDs hampered the rotation of surface groups and enhanced the π-conjugation, which reduces the nonradiative decay [33].

FL titrations were conducted to evaluate the sensitivity of the CDs-Tb ratiometric FL chromophore for DPA. We firstly investigated the influence of pH value from 5.19 to 9.06 in analyte solution on the DPA detection (Fig. S5). The FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of the CDs-Tb basically remained unchanged when the pH value was from 5.9 to 7.67, then fell rapidly with the pH value increasing. Taking this factor into account, pH 7.02 was recommended as the optimal pH value for further detection. Moreover, the enhancing process levelled off after 15 s with the extension of the incubation time (Fig. S6), indicating that the detection process was very rapid.

Fig. 3a showed the FL responses of the CDs-Tb when introducing different concentrations of DPA into aqueous solution. With the increase of the DPA concentration, the intensity of the emission band at 459 nm remained unchanged, meanwhile, an emission band at 495 appeared and the intensity of it greatly enhanced. Fig. 3b showed that the FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of the CDs-Tb was proportional to the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.5 nM to 2.5 μM. To our best knowledge, such a detection limit of 100 pM (3σ/K) is the lowest value than the best performance of a FL chromophore for detecting DPA reported so far ( Table 1). So high sensitivity was because that one carbon dot which was rich in carboxyl and amine groups could combine with many Tb3+ ions. That is to say, one CDs-Tb nanoprobe could bind with a lot of DPA ligands. As shown in Figs. 4a and 4b, the DPA couldn’t change the FL of pure CDs, but significantly enhanced the FL of free Tb3+, indicating that Tb3+ played an important role in sensitive detection of DPA. The new emission band at 495 nm was attributed to the 5D4→7F6 characteristic transition of Tb3+, which was caused by the efficient energy transfer from the DPA ligands to Tb3+ [3]. Therefore, the FL signal at 459 nm could act as a reference in the ratiometric chromophore to eliminate background interference and improve the detection sensitivity.

The DPA-induced fluorescent enhancement of CDs-Tb was highly specific. As shown in Fig. 5a, 10 μM common anion ions (Cl-, PO4 3-, HCO3 - and SO4 2-), reductants (L-Cys, G-SH and Vc), BA and CA, and nucleotides (ATP, CTP, UTP, GTP) were selected to study the FL performance of the CDs-Tb. Compared with the control group, FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of CDs-Tb was unchanged when adding interferents separately. Similarly, the amino acids had no influence on FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of CDs-Tb in the absence and presence of 1 μM DPA as well (Fig. 5b). In addition to that, the metal ions were also used to investigate the selectivity of DPA detection (Fig. S7). All the interferents had no effect on FL intensity ratio (I 495/I 459) of CDs-Tb in the presence and absence of DPA. Therefore, the proposed fluorescent ratiometry has great potential to be used for detecting DPA in the complex samples based on the high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the robustness of the detection system, we performed the DPA detection in orange juice and water samples. Different concentrations of standard DPA solutions (0.01, 0.1, 1 μM) were added into the CDs-Tb solution containing 10% running water as listed in Table 2. The reliability and accuracy of the detection method we developed were evaluated through a recovery study. For the orange juice and running water samples, the recoveries were about 103.34–105.26% and 93.99–106.35%, respectively, indicating that the assay was highly credible. Meanwhile, the low relative standard deviation (RSD, < 3.25%) demonstrated that the CDs-Tb could greatly improve the detection sensitivity.

With regard to application as a B. anthracis sensor in the demand for homeland security and public safety, simplicity and rapidity are highly desired as well. Taking this into account, we tried to develop a facile paper sensor for the DPA analysis. For this purpose, paper test strips were prepared by immersing the filter papers in water solution of CDs-Tb, which were then heat dried. When the paper test strips were immersed in DPA solution and dried, the color of the filter papers was white in the natural light, while the entire paper presented yellow FL under the 254 nm excitation ( Fig. 6a and b). As decrease of DPA concentration from 5000 to 0.5 nM, the yellow FL of the paper test strips gradually darken, but was still stronger than the control group visually even when the concentration of DPA as low as 0.5 nM. Meanwhile, the three-dimensional (3D) model (Fig. 6c) of the paper test strips were used to quantify the FL intensity by ImageJ software, which clearly showed that the FL of CDs-Tb could be significantly enhanced with the increase of DPA concentration. Therefore, the CDs-Tb-based paper sensor has a potential application for the visual detection of DPA due to its simplicity, rapidity and sensitivity.

In this study, an ultrasensitive detection approach of DPA was developed by using ratiometric fluorescent CDs-Tb probe. The DPA could enhance the FL of Tb3+ on the surface of CDs due to efficient energy transfer from the DPA ligands to Tb3+, but didn’t influence the FL of CDs. The ratiometric fluorescent method for DPA detection with the LOD of 100 pM, which is at least one order of magnitude lower than that of other methods. Most importantly, we successfully developed a facile, sensitive sensor for CDs-Tb-based paper sensor for DPA visual detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant no. 21535006) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2018C088).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.08.071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: This time it was real: knowledge of anthrax put to the test
